quiz.title: Unit 4 Quiz 2, Grade 11 Chemistry
quiz.subtitle: Bond Types and Electronegativity Differences
quiz.subject: Chemistry
quiz.topic: Hydrogen Bonding, Electrostatic Attraction, Electronegativity Differences, Ionic vs Covalent Bonds, Ion Formation, Metallic Bonding

questions.question: Which of the following is a hydrogen-bonded compound?
questions.explanation: H₂O (water) exhibits hydrogen bonding between its molecules. Hydrogen bonding occurs when hydrogen is bonded to highly electronegative atoms like oxygen, nitrogen, or fluorine. NaCl is ionic, CO₂ and O₂ are covalent but do not form hydrogen bonds.

questions.question: Which bond involves electrostatic attraction?
questions.explanation: Ionic bonds involve electrostatic attraction between oppositely charged ions. While metallic bonds also involve electrostatic attraction between positive ions and delocalized electrons, the question asks for a bond type, and ionic is the clearest example. Hydrogen bonds are also electrostatic but are intermolecular forces.

questions.question: If electronegativity difference is > 1.7, the bond is usually:
questions.explanation: An electronegativity difference greater than 1.7 typically indicates an ionic bond, where electrons are transferred rather than shared. This is a general guideline (Pauling scale) and works well for metal-nonmetal combinations.

questions.question: If electronegativity difference is 0–0.4, the bond is usually:
questions.explanation: An electronegativity difference of 0–0.4 indicates a nonpolar covalent bond, where electrons are shared almost equally. This typically occurs between identical atoms or atoms with very similar electronegativity.

questions.question: The bond in NaF is:
questions.explanation: NaF (sodium fluoride) has an ionic bond. Sodium (electronegativity ~0.9) transfers its electron to fluorine (electronegativity ~4.0), creating Na⁺ and F⁻ ions with a large electronegativity difference (~3.1).

questions.question: The bond in Cl₂ is:
questions.explanation: Cl₂ has a nonpolar covalent bond because both atoms are identical chlorine atoms with the same electronegativity, so electrons are shared equally.

questions.question: Ionic compounds form:
questions.explanation: Ionic compounds form crystalline lattice structures where each ion is surrounded by ions of opposite charge. They do not form discrete molecules like covalent compounds.

questions.question: Why do metals tend to lose electrons?
questions.explanation: Metals tend to lose electrons because they have low ionization energy — the energy required to remove an electron is relatively small. This allows them to easily form positive ions (cations). Metals typically have few valence electrons and achieve stable electron configurations (like noble gases) by losing them. High electronegativity (A) is characteristic of nonmetals, full outer shells (C) describe noble gases, and metals are actually stable as cations (D is false).

questions.question: Why do nonmetals tend to gain electrons?
questions.explanation: Nonmetals tend to gain electrons because they have high electronegativity — a strong attraction for electrons. They also have high electron affinity, meaning they release energy when gaining an electron. By gaining electrons, nonmetals achieve stable noble gas electron configurations. Low ionization energy (A) is characteristic of metals, empty inner shells (C) is not accurate, and nonmetals are not metallic (D is false).

questions.question: In metals, electrons are:
questions.explanation: In metallic bonding, electrons are delocalized, forming a 'sea' of electrons that move freely through the lattice of positive metal ions. This explains conductivity and malleability.

questions.question: Which type of bonding exists in NH₃?
questions.explanation: NH₃ (ammonia) has polar covalent bonds. Nitrogen is more electronegative than hydrogen, so bonding electrons are pulled toward nitrogen, creating partial charges.

questions.question: When sodium (Na) reacts with chlorine (Cl), what happens?
options: Sodium gains an electron from chlorine
Sodium loses an electron to chlorine
Chlorine loses a proton to sodium
questions.explanation: Sodium loses its valence electron to chlorine. Sodium becomes Na⁺ (cation), chlorine becomes Cl⁻ (anion), and they form an ionic bond in NaCl.

questions.question: What is formed when a metal loses electrons?
questions.explanation: When a metal loses electrons, it forms a positively charged ion called a cation. Metals have low ionization energy and tend to lose electrons to achieve stable electron configurations.

questions.question: What is formed when a nonmetal gains electrons?
questions.explanation: When a nonmetal gains electrons, it forms a negatively charged ion called an anion. Nonmetals have high electronegativity and tend to gain electrons to achieve stable octets.

questions.question: Which of the following has the strongest tendency to form an ionic bond?
questions.explanation: K (potassium) and Br (bromine) have the strongest tendency to form an ionic bond. K is a metal (Group 1) that readily loses an electron, and Br is a nonmetal (Group 17) that readily gains an electron. Their electronegativity difference is large. O and N, Cl and F are nonmetals that form covalent bonds. Ne is a noble gas and does not readily bond.

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You've mastered bond types and electronegativity trends! 🧪⚡
scoreMessages.70: Good job! Review the electronegativity difference guidelines for bond classification.
scoreMessages.50: Satisfactory. Focus on ion formation and what makes bonds ionic vs covalent.
scoreMessages.0: Please review bond types, electronegativity differences, and how ions form.
